coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive electrochemical sensor based on xylan-based Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite for dopamine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Guangda
Cai, Jihai
Liu, Chuanfu
Ren, Junli
Wang, Xiaohui
Yang, Jia
Wang, Xiaoying
coredata.dc:description: Although polysaccharide-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely used in the field of detection, most of the CQDs-based sensors only take advantage of their optical properties. In this work, considering the selectivity to target substance and reducibility of xylan-based CQDs, a high-sensitive sensor based on greenly synthesized CQDs-based nanocomposite was fabricated for dopamine (DA) detection. First, the CQDs prepared from xylan were used as the reductant and stabilizer to quickly synthesize silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and then Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite was obtained and loaded on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to fabricate electrochemical sensor. The electrochemical sensing of DA is of great significance for studying and treating neurochemical diseases due to its potential feasibility in diagnostics. Under optimal conditions, the linear range for monitoring DA was from 0.1 to 300 μM with a limit of detection of 1.59 nM (S/N = 3). Finally, this sensor was successfully applied to detect DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and bovine serum solution samples. The proposed strategy may broaden the application of polysaccharide-based CQDs and this sensor may provide a discriminative and sensitive analytical platform for DA clinical diagnostics and drug screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xylan-based CQDs
Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite
Detection
Dopamine
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposites modified electrode
Characterization of CQDs, Ag@CQDs and Ag@CQDs-rGO
Effects of scan rate and detection mechanism of DA
pH effects and DPV detection of DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) UV–vis spectra of Ag@CQDs composite prepared under different conditions of ratios (6 mg CQDs:0.35–0.5 mmol Tollens reagent), time (10–25 min) and temperatures (75–90 °C); (d) UV–vis spectra of Ag NPs reduced by xylan or CQDs.

(a) the UV–Vis spectra, (b) XRD patterns and (c) Raman spectra of GO, CQDs, CQDs-rGO and Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite; (d) the FT-IR spectra of xylan, CQDs, GO, CQDs-rGO and Ag@CQDs-rGO; (e) XPS spectrum of the Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite (f) Ag3d high-resolution XPS spectrum.
TEM images of (a) GO; (b) CQDs-rGO, the inset shows the presence of CQDs on rGO sheet; (c) Ag@CQDs, the inset is HRTEM image of Ag NPs; (d) Ag@CQDs-rGO, the inset is HRTEM image of CQDs.
(a) CV results of modified electrodes for DA (200 μM, scan rate 0.1 V s−1, pH of 6.5, 0.1 M PBS) and (b) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results of modified electrodes for DA (pH 6.5).

(a) CVs of 200 μM DA at Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH6.5) at different scan rates. (b) plot of the relationship between the peak redox current and the square root of the scan rate. (c) the relationship between the redox peak potential (Ep) and log v.

(a) CVs of 200 μM DA on Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE in different pH values PBS solution. (b) the relationship between the oxidation peak current and the oxidation peak potential of DA and the pH value. (c) DPV of DA with increasing concentration (from a to j: 0–300 μM). (d) the relationship between Ipa and the DA concentration.

(a) CVs obtained by Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE after adding 100 μM DA, AA, UA and glucose in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.5) with 0.1 V/s scan rate. (b) CVs of 100 μM DA recorded on the Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE with increasing time (from a to h: 0, 2,4,6,8,10,12,14 days).
Preparation processes of Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE for detection of DA.
analytical performances of some reported DA sensors.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive electrochemical sensor based on xylan-based Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite for dopamine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology, N-7491 Trondheim, Norway

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Norwegian University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology, N-7491 Trondheim, Norway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: a high-sensitivity electrochemical sensor based on green synthesized Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite has been developed to offer a new platform for the enhanced selectivity of dopamine detection.

Although polysaccharide-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely used in the field of detection, most of the CQDs-based sensors only take advantage of their optical properties. In this work, considering the selectivity to target substance and reducibility of xylan-based CQDs, a high-sensitive sensor based on greenly synthesized CQDs-based nanocomposite was fabricated for dopamine (DA) detection. First, the CQDs prepared from xylan were used as the reductant and stabilizer to quickly synthesize silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and then Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite was obtained and loaded on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to fabricate electrochemical sensor. The electrochemical sensing of DA is of great significance for studying and treating neurochemical diseases due to its potential feasibility in diagnostics. Under optimal conditions, the linear range for monitoring DA was from 0.1 to 300 μM with a limit of detection of 1.59 nM (S/N = 3). Finally, this sensor was successfully applied to detect DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and bovine serum solution samples. The proposed strategy may broaden the application of polysaccharide-based CQDs and this sensor may provide a discriminative and sensitive analytical platform for DA clinical diagnostics and drug screening.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is an essential neurotransmitter that transmits signals from the body to the brain and plays an important role in the central nervous system of mammals. It is responsible for various physiological events, including behavior, mood and movement [1,2]. Although DA itself is not a toxicant, an abnormal amount of DA is involved with some neurological diseases such as Parkinson’s disease and Schizophrenia [3]. Thus, there is an urgent demand to develop efficient, rapid, sensitive, and selective methods for DA detection. Up to now, there are various of sensing methods developed to detect DA, including high performance liquid chromatography [4], ion exchange chromatography [5], colorimetric method [6] and so on. However, these conventional methods are either poor selectivity, low sensitivity, or time consuming, requiring further separation processes [7]. Electrochemistry has attracted much attention for its reliability and effectiveness[8]. DA can be directly detected by this method based on its electrochemical activity. Nevertheless, the selectivity of DA detection on bare electrodes is affected by the coexistence of other interfering molecules with similar oxidation potentials such as ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) [9].

To solve these problems, various of materials have been used for the electrode modification. Owing to high conductivity, large specific surface area and good chemical stability, nanomaterials including metal nanoparticles [10,11], metal oxides [12,13]and carbon nanomaterials [14], have received much attention. Among metal nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are the most developed materials, which have been used to modify the surfaces of electrodes because of their catalytic effect and rapid electron transfer [15]. To further improve the electrocatalytic ability and selectivity of biosensors, a strategy of integrating metal nanoparticles with carbon materials is employed. Carbon materials have high electrical conductivity and large surface area such as carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, fiber carbon, and graphene oxide (GO), etc [16,17]. GO has high specific surface area and its two-dimensional structure also allows the existence of local p-conjugate-electrons on the surface [18]. Thanks to these unique attributes, GO is a good candidate as a promising catalyst support. In order to increase the sensor’s sensitivity, the strategy of combining more conductive reduced graphene oxide (rGO) with nanoparticles can be adopted. Mehmet Lutfi Yola et al.[19]confined gold nanoparticles on polyoxometalate/reduced graphene oxide nanohybrid (AuNPs/POM/rGO) for high-sensitive sensing of aqueous triclosan. rGO and metallic nanoparticles are synthesized by conventional reductants (such as hydrazine hydrate and sodium borohydride) in many reports [20], but these reductants are expensive and exhibit high toxicity resulting in serious hazards to environment. Therefore, there is a demand to develop an economical and eco-friendly synthetic method to make full use of the advantages of rGO and metal nanoparticles.

Polysaccharide-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a type of ‘zero-dimensional’ carbon materials recently have received widespread attention due to simple synthesis procedure, low toxicity and good solubility [21]. Most research has been focused on the fluorescence property of CQDs in the field of detection [22]. In fact, it is worthwhile taking full advantage of the other values of CQDs including excellent electrical conductivity and potential reducing properties. The surface of CQDs synthesized hydrothermally from polysaccharide has a large number of carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups [23]. On the one hand, ascribing to the negatively charged carboxyl, CQDs not only can stabilize the metal particles in aqueous solution [24], but also enhance the selectivity to certain substance through electrostatic interaction. On the other hand, CQDs could also function as green reducing agents due to the reducibility of hydroxyl groups [25]. So far, a few papers have reported that CQDs were used as a reducing agent to synthesize metallic nanoparticles. However, attempts to use CQDs to reduce GO without adding reductants have been rarely reported. Herein, xylan, a typical representative of polysaccharides, which has the advantages of low cost, rich carbon content and low carbonization temperature, is a promising carbon source for the prepartion of CQDs.

In this study, a high-sensitivity electrochemical sensor based on Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite for detection of DA was developed (Scheme 1 ). CQDs with xylan as the carbon source were used as the reductant and stabilizer to quickly synthesize Ag NPs and rGO under microwave conditions, respectively, and then Ag@CQDs were attached to CQDs-rGO to form Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposites through self-assembly. The synthetic method for Ag@CQDs-rGO was green and simple without any chemical reducing agent. The Ag NPs enable the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to be more conductive and electrocatalytic. And the DA interactions of rGO and CQDs were important factors to improve the selectivity of the sensor of DA. Under optimal conditions, a good linear relationship between the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) peak current and the DA concentration was observed in the range of 0.1 μM to 300 μM. Furthermore, the Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE was successfully used to detect DA content in clinical dopamine hydrochloride injection and bovine serum solution samples.
The chemicals and apparatus used in this experiment are introduced in the Supplementary materials.

1 g xylan was dissolved in 20 mL NaOH/urea (8%/12%, w/w) aqueous solution. The mixture was transferred to a 25 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with teflon and heated in a muffle furnace at 240 °C for 24 h. The resulting mixture solution was centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitate. After dialysis for a week with 500 Da MWCO, CQDs (0.25 mg/mL) were obtained and stored at room temperature away from light (seen in Scheme 1a).
Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from graphite powder by modified Hummers method [26].

Firstly, AgNO3 (0.68 g) was dissolved in the deionized water (10 mL), then ammonia was added dropwise until precipitate disappeared to obtain Tollens reagent. Next, 0.4 mmol of Tollens reagent was mixed with 30 mL of CQDs solution (0.25 mg/mL). Finally, the mixture was reacted at 80 °C for 20 min under microwave radiation (800 W). After dialysis for 5 days, a stable wine-red solution of Ag@CQDs was obtained. The effects of the mass ratio of CQDs to Tollens reagent (Ag@CQDs1 ~ 4), reaction time (Ag@CQDs5 ~ 9), and reaction temperature (Ag@CQDs10 ~ 12) on the generation of AgNPs were investigated. These samples are shown in Table S1.

5 mL of GO suspension (1 mg/mL) was added to 25 mL CQDs solution (0.25 mg/mL), then the mixture was reacted at 100 °C for 90 min under microwave radiation (800 W). After lyophilization, CQDs-rGO composite materials were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposites modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to use, a GCE with diameter of 3 mm was polished several times with Al2O3 paste to obtain a mirror like surface. The electrode was then sonicated in ethanol and deionized water, followed by drying in a purified nitrogen stream. 1 mg of CQDs-rGO powder was added to 2 mL of Ag@CQDs solution and stirred for 60 min to form a uniformly dispersed Ag@CQDs-rGO solution. After that, the obtained suspension (15.0 μL) was dropped to the surface of cleaned GCE and dried in air at room temperature. In the same way, GO, rGO, and CQDs-rGO were used to modify GCE, respectively. The schematic illustration of Ag@CQDs-rGO preparation and electrode modification are shown in Scheme 1.
The detection of real samples in this experiment are introduced in the Supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, Ag@CQDs and Ag@CQDs-rGO
ce:section.ce:para: The micrographs of CQDs (Fig. S1a and b) were characterized by TEM and AFM. CQDs were monodispersed quasi-sphere and their size were about 5 nm. The 0.24 nm graphite lattice fringes corresponding to the (1120) plane were clearly visible in the HR-TEM image of the CQDs [27,28]. This confirms a certain crystallinity of CQDs. In addition, AFM shows that the height of CQDs was about 0.5 nm, suggesting that the as-prepared CQDs were consisted of few-layer graphene sheets.

The formation of silver nanoparticles by using CQDs as reducing and stabilizing agent was studied through UV–vis spectra. A strong absorption peak centered at 420 nm can be observed in Fig. 1 , which is related to the characteristic surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak of Ag NPs [29]. As shown in Fig. 1a, when we used 0.35 mmol Tollens reagent as the silver ion source, the absorption peak was weak, indicating that few Ag NPs were produced under this condition. As the concentration of Tollens reagent increased, a narrower band with higher intensity was clearly observed in the UV–vis, which indicates that more Ag NPs with a narrow size distribution were formed. And when Tollens reagent was 0.40 mmol, the maximum absorption was observed. However, when the concentration of Tollens reagent was further increased, the absorption band became weak, broadened and red-shifted, indicating that Ag NPs had started to aggregate [30]. Therefore, we chose 6 mg CQDs:0.40 mmol Tollens reagent as the appropriate mass ratio in our experiments. In Fig. 1b, we discuss the effect of reaction time on the synthesis of Ag NPs. When the reaction time was from 10 min to 20 min, the maximum absorption peak varied between 422.93 nm and 416.37 nm. The small blue shift was related to the change of particle size distribution, indicating that the nanoparticles became more spherical with the increase of reaction time [30]. However, the absorption in the ultraviolet–visible spectrum decreased when the microwave heating reaction time was further prolonged, suggesting that excessive reaction time might cause Ag NPs to aggregate. In this case, the Ag@CQDs prepared composite was unstable. Therefore, the best reaction time was 20 min in this experiment. Fig. 1c shows the UV–vis spectrum of Ag NPs prepared at different temperatures. The absorption peak was weak when the temperature was 75 °C, indicating that Ag NPs were rarely formed. When the reaction temperature increased to 80 °C, the absorption of the UV–vis spectra reached its maximum. However, a further increase in the reaction temperature resulted in a decrease in absorption. The reason might be that more energy made Ag NPs grow too fast at relatively high temperature [31], which might cause larger diameters and smaller amounts of Ag NPs, thus weakening the strength of absorption peak. Therefore, we chose 80 °C as the optimal reaction temperatures in this experiment. Fig. 1d shows the UV–vis spectra of Ag NPs prepared using xylan or CQDs as the reductant and stabilizer under the same conditions. The results show that the effect of CQDs on reducing Ag NPs was better than that of xylan. It could also be found that the Ag NPs solution reduced by CQDs was burgundy, and the Ag NPs solution reduced by xylan was light yellow.

Fig. S2a, b and c show the size distribution of Ag NPs prepared under optimal ratio (6 mg CQDs:0.40 mmol Tollens reagent), reaction time (20 min) and reaction temperature (80 °C), respectively. And the size distribution result of Ag@CQDs10 suggests that the average particle size of Ag NPs was relatively smaller, which was in agreement with the UV–vis spectroscopy results.

The UV–visible spectra of GO, CQDs, CQDs-rGO and Ag@CQDs-rGO are shown in Fig. 2 a. In the UV–Vis spectrum of GO, a strong and a weak characteristic peaks could be observed at 234 and 304 nm, which were attributed to π-π* transition of the aromatic C = C bonds and the n-π* transition of C = O bonds, respectively [32]. CQDs also showed a characteristic peak at 229 nm, which was attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic C = C bond [33]. However, the UV absorption peaks of CQDs-rGO near 304 nm disappeared, indicating that GO was reduced by CQDs. Compared with CQDs-rGO, Ag@CQDs-rGO had one more absorption peak near 420 nm, indicating that it contained Ag NPs.

The crystal properties and phase structure of the prepared sample were confirmed by XRD analysis (Fig. 2b). The diffraction of GO showed a sharp peak at 2θ = 9.78° corresponded to (001) plane. A wide peak centered at 26° was appeared in the XRD patterns of the CQDs for its highly disordered carbon structure [33]. For CQDs-rGO, a strong broad peak at 25.4° and a very small peak at 43.3° appeared, corresponding to the (002) and (111) planes of rGO [34], respectively. This result further suggested the reduction of GO sheets by the CQDs. In hybrid Ag@CQDs-rGO composite, the appearance of major peaks at 38.1°, 44.3°, 64.5°, 77.3° and 81.6° were assigned to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes of Ag NPs with the face-centered cubic structure [31].

Raman spectra of the samples are exhibited in Fig. 2c. The pristine GO exhibited two characteristic bands, D band (1350 cm−1)and G band (1585 cm−1) respectively. The D band originates from a breathing κ-point phonon with A1g symmetry and it is related to local defects and disorders, whereas the G band is assigned to the E2g mode of sp2-bonded carbon atoms [35]. Raman studies of CQDs showed contributions from both the G band at 1590 cm−1 related to in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon and the D band at 1355 cm−1 related to the existence of sp3 defects [36]. Compared with GO, the D and G bands of CQDs-rGO shifted to lower wavenumbers at 1345 and 1576 cm-1 due to the recovery of sp2 domain [36]. And the D and G bands of Ag@CQDs-rGO also shifted to lower wavenumbers at 1335 and 1581 cm−1. The strength ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) is related to the average size of sp2 domain. The higher the ID/IG ratio exhibits, the smaller the size of the sp2 domain is. The ID/IG ratios of GO, CQDs, CQDs-rGO and Ag@CQDs-rGO were 1.042, 1.003, 1.163 and 1.162, respectively. It was reasonable that the ID/IG ratios of CQDs-rGO and Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite were greater than that of GO, because the conjugated graphene network (sp2 carbon) could be re-established after GO reduction, but the size of the re-constructed graphene network was usually smaller than the original graphite layer, resulting in an increase of ID/IG ratio [37]. Thus, it was confirmed that GO was reduced by CQDs. In addition, mixing silver nanoparticles on the surface of CQDs-rGO might increase the intensity peaks of the D and G bands owing to the surface-enhanced Raman scattering effect caused by the strong local electromagnetic field of nanoparticles [37].

The FT-IR spectra of xylan, CQDs, GO, CQDs-rGO and Ag@CQDs-rGO are presented in Fig. 2d, the typical absorption band of xylan at 3430 cm−1, 2923 cm−1, 1687 cm−1 were ascribed to bending vibration of O-H, stretching vibration of C-H, C = O, respectively. The characteristic absorption band of xylan at 1055 cm−1 was assigned to C-O-C in glycosidic bond. The characteristic absorption band of xylan at 895 cm−1 was originated from β-D-glycoside bond between sugar units [38]. Compared with xylan, the FT-IR spectra of CQDs at 1408 cm−1 (C-OH) and 1687 cm−1(C = O) were significantly enhanced, and C-O single bonds at 1055 cm−1 were weakened. This might be one of the reasons why CQDs could better reduce silver ions and GO. The FT-IR spectrum of GO showed the characteristic bands related to –OH (3000–3500 cm−1), C-H (2923 cm−1), C-OH (1408 cm−1), C-O (1055 cm−1) and C = O(1687 cm−1) [32]. The FT-IR spectroscopy of CQDs-rGO shows the reduced intensity of all the bands associated with the oxygen-containing functional groups and mainly exhibits two absorption bands at 1408 cm−1 (C-OH) and 1055 cm−1(C-O), which suggests that GO was reduced successfully by CQDs. After the Ag@CQDs deposition on the surface of the CQDs-rGO nanosheets, the FT-IR spectrum of the Ag@CQDs-rGO composite remained almost similar to CQDs-rGO, but the band intensity decreased. Furthermore, in the FT-IR spectroscopy of Ag@CQDs-rGO, the band at 1687 cm−1 (C = O) disappeared, indicating interaction of silver nanoparticles with the graphene [39].

As shown in the full scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e), four typical peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.8 eV, 370.2 eV and 530.8 eV were assigned to C1s, N1s , Ag3d and O1s, respectively. Fig. 2f shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of the Ag 3d peaks of Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite. There were two peaks of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 centered 366.4 eV and 372.4 eV. Additionally, the difference in the binding energy between the two doublets was observed to be 6 eV, which was indicative of Ag NPs on the surface of the CQDs-rGO sheets [11].

The TEM and SEM images of GO are shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S3a. From the image, it could be observed that GO had a few stacking folds due to the distortions from the high fraction of sp3 C-O bonds [32]. It is further observed from the Fig. 3b that there was a certain degree of accumulation between graphene layers in CQDs-rGO. This was owing to the elimination of oxygen-containing groups in GO during the reduction of GO by CQDs, which leaded to the restacking of rGO sheets under π-π interaction [40]. In the inset of Fig. 3b, it is found that there were many CQDs of about 5 nm on the rGO sheet. As shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S3b, the spherical Ag NPs were well dispersed with diameters of 25 ± 5 nm. Furthermore, Fig. 3c showed a lot of CQDs around Ag NPs. HRTEM further characterizes the structure of Ag@CQDs (the inset of Fig. 3c), the image shows an observed lattice spacing of 0.238 nm which was in agreement with the (111) facets of Ag crystal [15]. Fig. 3d and Fig. S3c show the TEM and SEM images of Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite. The spherical silver nanoparticles were distributed uniformly on the rGO sheets and the aggregation of nanoparticles could be effectively prevented when rGO was used as the substrate. While, the restacking of graphene nanosheets could also be avoided by the attachment of Ag nanoparticles on the rGO surface. In addition, a 0.24 nm lattice fringe around Ag NPs is evident, which is consistent with the (1120) plane of CQDs (the inset of Fig. 3d).

In the hydrothermal reaction of xylan, NaOH/urea was chosen as a better solvent to dissolve xylan than NaOH, meanwhile nitrogen doping CQDs was achieved. Elemental mapping of Ag@CQDs is shown in Fig. S4. The green represents Ag NPs (Fig. S4c), and the red represents nitrogen in CQDs (Fig. S4d). The elemental maps of Ag and N for the Ag NPs aggregates show that the CQDs were abundant around the surface of the Ag NPs (Fig. S4b). This result also provides a strong evidence in favor of the possibility of Ag+ reduced by CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CV plot of bare GCE showed a reduction peak of 2.945 μA and an oxidation peak current of −3.208 μA. After casting GO on the GCE, the redox peaks weakened. This might be that GO contained lots of oxygen-containing functional groups, which resulted in poor GO/GCE conductivity. When the rGO was covered on the same electrode, the oxidation and reduction currents was increased by 284.4% and 272.4%, respectively, which indirectly indicates that the oxygen-containing functional groups of GO could adversely affect the conductivity. When the CQDs-rGO was fixed on the electrode surface, and the redox peak was also slightly increased due to the excellent conductivity of CQDs. For Ag@CQDs-rGO modified the electrode, the redox peaks increased significantly, which indicated that the sensitivity was the highest among these modified electrodes because the higher current means higher sensitivity for DA with the constant concentration. Obviously, Ag@CQDs-rGO was beneficial to improve the electrocatalytic redox reaction of DA, which may be due to its excellent catalytic activity and electron conductivity.

Fig. 4b represents the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of different modified GCEs at an open circuit potential containing 200 μM DA in 0.1 m PBS at a frequency range of 0.1–105 Hz and a voltage amplitude of 5 mV. In the Nyquist plots (Fig. 4b, inset), the electron transfer resistance (Rct) could be estimated from the diameter of the higher frequency semicircular domain. The Rct value was calculated to be 76 Ω of Ag@CQDs-rGO modified the GCE, which was lower than those of the bare GCE, the GCE modified by GO, the GCE modified by rGO and the GCE modified by CQDs-rGO. This decrease of Rct is mainly due to the good conductivity of Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of scan rate and detection mechanism of DA
ce:section.ce:para:
The influence of scan rate on current and potential was also evaluated, and the curve are shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a shows the CVs of 200 μM DA at Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE with different scan rates. With the increasing scan rate from 0.025 to 0.2 V/s, the redox current gradually increased. Between the redox peak current and the square root of the scan rate, two good linear relationships (R = 0.994, R = 0.992, respectively) were found in Fig. 5b, indicating that the electrochemical reaction of DA oxidation is a typical diffusion-controlled electron transfer process at Ag@CQDs-rGO modified electrode. As the scan rate increased, the oxidation potential of DA moved positively. The dynamic parameters could be calculated by Laviron’s equation [41]:
(1)
E
p c
=
E
0 '
-
2.3 R T
α n F
l o g ν
(2)
E
p a
=
E
0 '
- +
2.3 R T
( 1 - α ) n F
l o g ν
(3)
log
κ s
= α log
1 - α
+
1 - α
log
α -
log
RT
nF ν
-
1 - α
α
nF Δ Ep
2.3 RT
where n is the number of electrons transferred, κs is the electron-transfer rate constant and the others have their usual meanings. In general, in electrochemical reactions, the value of α, n can be easily calculated from the linear regression equations: Epa(V) = 0.05498logν(V/s) + 0.3761(R = 0.998), Epc(V) = -0.05381 log ν(V/s) + 0.09413(R = 0.996), which is shown in Fig. 5(c). The values of α and n were determined as 0.51 and 2.1(≈2), respectively. The value of κs was 0.36, which was higher than some previously studies [42], illustrating that Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE could improve electron transfer dynamics of DA in this work.

The detection mechanism for DA is proposed in Scheme 1. In the PBS buffer solution at pH 6.5, the positively charged aromatic DA molecules could easily approach the sensing interface of Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE through π-π stacking and/or electrostatic attraction with the Ag@CQDs-rGO, and produce large electrochemical response. During anode scanning, DA was electrochemically oxidized to form dopamine-phthalic acid (DAQ) under Ag@CQDs-rGO catalysis, and released two protons and two electrons [43–45]. Meanwhile, the Faraday current generated by the electrons on the electrode surface depended on the concentration of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effects and DPV detection of DA
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH values of 0.1 M PBS (from 5.0 to 8.5) on the electrochemical behaviors of Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE toward DA was investigated. In Fig. 6 a-b, the peak current and potential of DA were strongly affected by pH, indicating that protons were involved in the electrode reaction process. A good linear relationship between Epa and pH was established by the regression equation of Epa (V) = -0.05306pH + 0.66201(R = 0.995). The K value of −0.05306 V/pH is close to the theoretical value of 0.059 V/pH, indicating that the electron transfer was accompanied by an equal number of protons [46]. Meanwhile, the peak current of DA increased with the increasing pH and decreased after reaching 6.5. Therefore, to ensure the sensitive determination for DA, pH value of 6.5 was chosen as the supporting electrolyte for the subsequent analytical experiments.

The more sensitive DPV was used to study the determination of DA. As shown in Fig. 6c, the peak current (Ipa) of DA increased gradually with the adding of DA under the optimal conditions. In Fig. 6d, the relationship between the peak current and DA concentration (from 0.1 μM to 300 μM) could be described with a good linear regression equations of Ipa (μA) = -0.106C (μM)-4.214(R = 0.999). Furthermore, the detection limit (S/N = 3) was estimated to be 1.59 nM, lower than many previous reports (seen in Table 1 ), indicating Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE had better sensitivity and a wider linear range.

Ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) could easily affect DA detection on bare GCE due to their overlapping signals [51]. This problem could be solved by modifying the electrode with Ag@CQDs-rGO. In the PBS buffer solution at pH 6.5, AA (pKa = 4.1) and UA (pKa = 5.75) were negatively charged, DA (pKa = 8.89) solution at pH = 6.5 was positively charged [52]. CQDs had negatively charged carboxyl groups [24], which facilitated the passage of DA molecules and prevented negatively charged AA and UA from reaching the electrode surface. Furthermore, compared with AA, UA and glucose, DA has a special aromatic ring that could form a π-π conjugate with rGO, which made DA easier to reach the electrode surface than these interfering substances to enhance the selectivity of dopamine detection. As shown in Fig. 7 a, there was no significant change in the current signal after 100 μM DA, AA, UA and glucose injection. Therefore, common interfering substances like AA, UA and glucose do not interfere DA detection by modifying the electrode with Ag@CQDs-rGO.

The stability of Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE was also studied by exposing the electrode into air at room temperature for 14 days. In Fig. 7b, the peak current of DA only decay by less than 4.5%, revealing wonderful stability.

In order to illustrate the reliability of the developed sensor in clinical applications, the experiments of actual sample analysis were also studied through standard addition methods. The diluted dopamine hydrochloride injection and bovine serum samples were taken as real samples, and the analysis results are shown in Table S2 and Table S3, respectively. The recoveries were between 97.2% and 102.6% and RSD (n = 3) were less than 2.9%. The data implies that the sensor of Ag@CQDs-rGO/GCE had a potential ability to detect DA in real samples.

In summary, a green and simple method was developed to synthesize CQDs using xylan as a carbon source. Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite was easily obtained by using CQDs as the reducing agent and stabilizer through a simple sequential reduction strategy. And then Ag@CQDs-rGO nanocomposite loaded on GCE to fabricate an efficient and sensitive sensor for detection of dopamine. Notably, the CQDs prepared from xylan not only improve conductivity of electrode but also enable highly selective reorganization of dopamine through electrostatic interaction between amine functional groups and carboxyl groups. This sensor based on Ag@CQDs-rGO broadened the application of polysaccharide-based CQDs in electrochemical sensor.

Han Guangda: Data collection and curation, Writing original draft preparation, Experimental measurement. Cai Jihai: Investigation, Software. Liu Chuanfu: Investigation, Revising the paper. Ren Junli: Revising the paper. Wang Xiaohui: Revising the paper. Yang Jia: Provide the instrument. Wang Xiaoying: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148566.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
